questions.question: In the 30-Day Action Plan, which should be your Week 1 priority?

options.text: Test all of your quizzes with keyboard navigation
Check all of your videos for captions
Fix your syllabus and one high-use document
Share what you've learned with a colleague

questions.explanation: The 30-Day Action Plan starts with the syllabus in Week 1 because the syllabus is the most-read document in a course. It has immediate, high impact. Fixing your syllabus and one other frequently-used document creates accessibility at the foundation of your course from day one. Weeks 2, 3, and 4 then add quizzes, videos, and broader engagement.

questions.question: Why is accessibility considered an ongoing process rather than a one-time fix?

options.text: Because new tools keep being released and you have to learn them all
Because standards evolve (WCAG 3.0 is coming), course materials change each semester, and new content needs evaluation
Because there is no such thing as truly accessible materials
Because accessibility is more important than good teaching

questions.explanation: Accessibility is ongoing because: (1) Web Content Accessibility Guidelines evolve (WCAG 3.0 is in development), (2) you update your course materials every semester (new readings, new videos, redesigned assessments), and (3) new tools and third-party content emerge and require evaluation. You're not learning everything in perpetuity—you stay current by following 2-3 accessibility sources and attending your institution's annual training. But accessibility is never truly "done."
